all_items: ['Unexplained Weight Loss: Significant weight loss (e.g., $10$ pounds or more) without trying can be an early indicator.', 'Fatigue: Extreme, persistent tiredness that does not improve with rest.', 'Lumps or Thickening: New or unusual lumps, especially in the breast, testicles, or lymph nodes.', 'Skin Changes: New moles, changes in existing moles (e.g., asymmetry or jagged borders), or sores that do not heal.', 'Unexplained Bleeding: Blood in urine or stool, unusual vaginal bleeding, or coughing up blood.', 'Persistent Changes: Nagging cough, ongoing hoarseness, or sudden, long-lasting changes in bowel or bladder habits. American Cancer Society +3']

all_items: ['Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC): The most common type, accounting for roughly 85% of cases. It includes subtypes like adenocarcinoma and squamous cell carcinoma.', 'Small Cell Lung Cancer (SCLC): Less common (~15% of cases) but typically more aggressive and fast-growing. Cleveland Clinic +1', 'A persistent or worsening cough', 'Coughing up blood', 'Unexplained chest pain or shortness of breath', 'Unexplained weight loss and fatigue', 'Recurrent respiratory infections (like bronchitis or pneumonia) World Health Organization (WHO)', 'Tobacco Smoking: The leading cause, linked to roughly 60–70% of cases. This includes cigarettes, cigars, and passive smoking.', 'Environmental & Occupational Exposure: Includes inhaling radon gas, asbestos, arsenic, and chronic exposure to high levels of air pollution. Mayo Clinic +4', 'Early-Stage: Primary treatments often involve surgical removal of the tumor (lobectomy or wedge resection), sometimes paired with targeted therapies.', 'Locally Advanced: Often treated with a combination of chemotherapy, radiation therapy, and immunotherapy.', 'Advanced (Metastatic): Focuses on prolonging survival and improving quality of life using targeted therapies, immunotherapies, and chemotherapy. World Health Organization (WHO) +1']

all_items: ["Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC): The most common type. It typically grows slowly and rarely spreads. It often appears as a pearly bump, a pinkish patch, or a sore that doesn't heal.", 'Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC): Often appears as a firm red nodule, a flat lesion with a scaly crust, or a rough patch. It is more likely to spread than BCC if left untreated.', 'Melanoma: The most aggressive and dangerous form of skin cancer. It is highly likely to spread to other parts of the body, making rapid identification vital. Mayo Clinic +5', 'A - Asymmetry: One half of the mole does not match the other half.', 'B - Border: The edges are irregular, scalloped, or poorly defined.', 'C - Color: The color is not uniform and may include different shades of brown or black, or patches of pink, red, or white.', 'D - Diameter: The mole is larger than 6mm (about the size of a pencil eraser), although melanomas can be smaller.', 'E - Evolution: The mole is changing in size, shape, color, or elevation. You should also watch for symptoms like new itching, tenderness, or bleeding.', 'Wearing sunscreen: Use a broad-spectrum sunscreen with an SPF of 30 or higher daily.', 'Protective clothing: Wear wide-brimmed hats, UV-blocking sunglasses, and long-sleeved clothing.', 'Avoiding tanning beds: Indoor tanning significantly increases UV exposure and cancer risk.', 'Seeking shade: Limit direct sun exposure, especially between 10 AM and 4 PM. Mayo Clinic +1']

all_items: ['Changes in bowel habits: Persistent new-onset constipation, diarrhea, or narrow, "pencil-thin" stools.', 'Rectal bleeding: Bright red or very dark blood in the stool.', 'Abdominal discomfort: Frequent gas, bloating, cramps, or an ongoing feeling that your bowel does not empty completely.', 'Unexplained anemia: Iron-deficiency anemia, which often results in chronic fatigue or weakness due to slow, unseen bleeding.', 'Unexplained weight loss: Losing weight without trying. National Cancer Institute (.gov) +3', 'When to start: The American Cancer Society and other health organizations recommend that average-risk adults begin colorectal cancer screening at age 45.', 'Screening methods: A Colonoscopy is considered the gold standard, as it can both screen and remove polyps during the same procedure. Other options include at-home stool tests (such as FIT or mt-sDNA tests) or visual exams like a CT colonography. Wiley +1', 'Age: While rates are rising in younger adults, the majority of cases occur in people over 45.', 'Family history: A family history of colorectal cancer or certain types of polyps.', 'Lifestyle factors: A diet high in red or processed meats, obesity, physical inactivity, smoking, and heavy alcohol consumption.', "Medical history: Having inflammatory bowel disease (like ulcerative colitis or Crohn's disease), or type 2 diabetes. Mayo Clinic +2", 'Surgery: Often the primary treatment, involving the removal of the cancerous section of the colon.', 'Chemotherapy and Radiation: Used to destroy cancer cells or shrink tumors, often before surgery or to prevent recurrence.', 'Targeted and Immunotherapy: Advanced therapies designed to target specific genetic mutations in the cancer cells. National Cancer Institute (.gov) +1']

all_items: ['Cancer Surgery: A procedure to physically remove the tumor and surrounding tissue.', 'Radiation Therapy: High-powered energy beams (like X-rays) used to kill cancer cells and shrink tumors.', 'Chemotherapy: Systemic drugs used to kill or stop the growth of fast-dividing cancer cells. National Cancer Institute (.gov) +1', 'Immunotherapy: Treatments designed to help your own immune system recognize and fight cancer cells.', 'Targeted Drug Therapy: Medications that target specific gene changes, proteins, or the tissue environment that allows cancer to grow.', 'Hormone Therapy: Treatments that slow or stop the growth of cancers (like certain breast or prostate cancers) that rely on hormones.', 'Stem Cell Transplant: Procedures that restore blood-forming cells destroyed by high doses of chemotherapy or radiation. National Cancer Institute (.gov) +1', 'Palliative Care: Specialized medical care focused on providing relief from pain and other symptoms to improve quality of life. Mayo Clinic +1']

all_items: ["The Mechanism: Normally, insulin helps your cells use blood sugar for energy. In Type 2, the cells don't respond normally to insulin (insulin resistance), leading your pancreas to eventually produce less of it.", 'Diagnosis: Doctors usually diagnose this using an A1C test, which measures average blood sugar over the past 2–3 months. An A1C level of $\\ge6.5\\%$ on two separate tests indicates diabetes.', 'Common Symptoms: Many develop the condition gradually with mild symptoms. Look out for excessive thirst, frequent urination, unexplained fatigue, blurred vision, or slow-healing sores. MedlinePlus (.gov) +3', 'Being overweight or having obesity', 'A sedentary lifestyle (lack of physical activity)', 'Family history or genetics', 'Being age 35 or older (though it is increasingly common in younger demographics)', 'A history of prediabetes or gestational diabetes MedlinePlus (.gov) +2', 'Dietary Changes: Focusing on whole grains, lean proteins, vegetables, and limiting processed sugars.', "Physical Activity: Regular exercise improves your body's sensitivity to insulin.", 'Medication: If lifestyle changes are not enough, oral medications or insulin therapy may be prescribed to control blood sugar. YouTube·Mass General Brigham +1', 'Calculate & Track: Use tools like the CDC Diabetes Risk Calculator to assess your personal risk factors.', 'Official Guidelines: Review comprehensive management guides provided by the American Diabetes Association.', 'Global Health Insights: For international statistics and fact sheets, explore the World Health Organization (WHO) or the International Diabetes Federation.']

all_items: ['Type 1 Diabetes: An autoimmune condition where the body attacks its own pancreas, stopping it from producing insulin. It requires daily insulin therapy and usually begins in childhood or young adulthood.', "Type 2 Diabetes: The most common form, where the body becomes resistant to insulin or doesn't make enough. It is strongly linked to genetics, weight, and lifestyle.", 'Gestational Diabetes: Develops in some women during pregnancy and usually resolves after the baby is born, though it increases the risk of later developing Type 2.', 'Extreme thirst and frequent urination', 'Unexplained weight loss (often in Type 1)', 'Extreme fatigue and weakness', 'Blurry vision Wikipedia +1', 'Lifestyle Changes: Healthy diet modifications (focusing on low glycemic index foods) and regular physical activity.', 'Monitoring: Using tools like glucose meters or continuous glucose monitors (CGMs) to track blood sugar levels.', 'Medication: Oral medications to improve insulin sensitivity, or daily insulin injections. Cleveland Clinic +2']

all_items: ['Energy Production: After you eat, carbohydrates are broken down into glucose, which enters your bloodstream. This signals your pancreas to release insulin.', 'Blood Sugar Control: Insulin acts like a key, unlocking cells in your muscles, fat, and liver so glucose can enter and be used or stored. This process prevents blood sugar from reaching dangerously high levels. Cleveland Clinic +4', 'Rapid-Acting (Bolus): Starts working in about 15 minutes and covers meals; typically taken before eating.', 'Short-Acting: Takes effect within 30 to 60 minutes and lasts for several hours.', 'Intermediate-Acting: Starts working in 1 to 3 hours and manages blood sugar for the half-day.', 'Long-Acting (Basal): Provides a steady, background level of insulin that lasts for up to 24 hours.', 'Inhaled Insulin: A powdered form taken via an inhaler for adults with diabetes. National Institutes of Health (.gov) +1', 'Insulin Syringes and Vials', 'Insulin Pens (pre-filled or reusable)', 'Insulin Pumps (wearable devices that deliver continuous insulin)', 'Diabetes Mellitus: A chronic condition where the body does not make enough insulin (Type 1) or becomes resistant to it (Type 2).', 'Insulin Resistance: A condition where muscle, fat, and liver cells do not respond effectively to insulin, often leading to prediabetes. National Institutes of Health (.gov) +1']

all_items: ['Increased Thirst & Dry Mouth: Excess sugar buildup draws fluid from tissues, making you feel constantly thirsty.', 'Frequent Urination: Drinking more fluids naturally leads to more bathroom trips, including waking up several times a night.', 'Unexplained Weight Loss: Despite eating more to satisfy hunger, your body may burn fat and muscle for energy instead of glucose.', 'Extreme Fatigue: Your body cannot effectively use sugar for energy, leaving you chronically exhausted.', 'Blurry Vision: Fluid shifts inside your eye can change its shape and cause temporary blurriness. YouTube·Cleveland Clinic +2', 'Slow-Healing Sores: Minor cuts, scrapes, or blisters taking weeks or months to heal.', 'Frequent Infections: Unusually high recurrence of skin, gum, or vaginal yeast infections.', 'Numbness or Tingling: A loss of sensation or a "pins and needles" feeling, typically in the hands or feet.', 'Darkened Skin Patches: Small, rough, dark patches of skin (acanthosis nigricans) commonly found on the neck or armpits. Mayo Clinic +4', 'Nausea, vomiting, or abdominal pain', 'Fruity-smelling breath', 'Shortness of breath', 'Confusion or fainting National Institutes of Health (.gov)']

all_items: ['Diet: Adopt the DASH (Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension) diet, which emphasizes fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and lean protein.', 'Sodium Reduction: Limit salt (sodium) intake to under 2,000 mg daily, ideally aiming for 1,500 mg.', 'Physical Activity: Engage in at least 30 minutes of moderate aerobic exercise (such as brisk walking) on most days of the week.', 'Weight Management: Maintain a healthy weight; even a modest weight loss can significantly lower blood pressure.', 'Limit Alcohol & Smoking: Restrict alcohol consumption and avoid tobacco use. National Institutes of Health (.gov) +3', 'Diuretics ("Water Pills"): Help the kidneys remove excess sodium and water from the body, reducing blood volume and pressure. Common examples include chlorthalidone and hydrochlorothiazide.', 'ACE Inhibitors: Relax blood vessels by preventing the formation of a natural chemical that narrows them.', 'ARBs (Angiotensin II Receptor Blockers): Relax blood vessels by blocking the action—rather than the formation—of that same narrowing chemical.', 'Calcium Channel Blockers (CCBs): Prevent calcium from entering the muscle cells of your heart and blood vessels, allowing blood vessels to relax and open.', "Beta-Blockers: Reduce the heart rate and the heart's workload, while also blocking certain stress hormones. Mayo Clinic +3"]

National Institutes of Health (.gov) +4', 'Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension (IIH): Also known as pseudotumor cerebri. The cause is unknown, but it is heavily associated with obesity, polycystic ovarian syndrome, and predominantly affects women of childbearing age.', 'Secondary Intracranial Hypertension: Caused by underlying medical emergencies or conditions, including severe head injuries, stroke, meningitis, brain tumors, blood clots (venous sinus thrombosis), or certain medications. nhs.uk +4', 'Imaging: Head CT or MRI scans.', 'Lumbar Puncture (Spinal Tap): Measures the exact pressure of the CSF and checks its composition. A pressure reading greater than $250\\text{ mmH}_2\\text{O}$ is usually indicative of IIH in adults. National Institutes of Health (.gov) +2', 'Medical Management: Weight loss and medications like acetazolamide (which reduces CSF production) are the standard first-line therapies.', 'Surgical Options: If vision deteriorates or symptoms are severe, procedures such as optic nerve sheath fenestration, CSF shunting (draining fluid to the abdomen), or venous sinus stenting may be required. National Institutes of Health (.gov)']

all_items: ['Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD): Chronic, exaggerated worry and tension about everyday events, seemingly without specific triggers.', 'Social Anxiety Disorder: Intense fear of being watched, judged, or embarrassed in social situations.', 'Panic Disorder: Sudden, repeated episodes of intense fear accompanied by physical symptoms like heart palpitations or dizziness (panic attacks).', 'Phobias: An irrational and intense fear of specific objects or situations (e.g., heights, spiders, flying). World Health Organization (WHO) +2', 'Psychological: Feeling restless, irritable, or having a constant sense of impending danger or doom.', 'Cognitive: Difficulty concentrating, racing thoughts, or inability to stop worrying.', 'Physical: Rapid heart rate, shortness of breath, sweating, trembling, nausea, or unexplained muscle tension. World Health Organization (WHO) +1', 'Routine & Sleep: Maintain regular sleeping and eating habits.', 'Limit Stimulants: Cut down on caffeine and alcohol, as they can exacerbate jitteriness and panic.', 'Move Your Body: Incorporate regular exercise, such as a brisk walk or yoga, to release physical tension.', 'Practice Mindfulness: Use deep breathing exercises, meditation, or grounding techniques (like the 5-4-3-2-1 method) to anchor yourself in the present moment. all_items: ['Uterine Healing: You will experience cramping and a bloody vaginal discharge (lochia) as your uterus shrinks back to its normal size.', 'Breast Changes: As your milk comes in, breasts can become swollen, tight, and sore.', 'General Discomfort: Soreness (especially from vaginal tears or a C-section incision) and night sweats are common.', 'The 5-5-5 Rule: A popular recovery guideline suggests: 5 days in bed, 5 days around the bed (minimal walking), and 5 days around the house to ensure proper healing. Cleveland Clinic +4', 'Baby Blues: Many parents experience mood swings, anxiety, or sadness in the first two weeks. These usually resolve on their own.', "Postpartum Depression (PPD): If feelings of severe sadness, hopelessness, anxiety, or disconnection from your baby last longer than two weeks, it could be PPD. This is a serious but highly treatable condition. Office on Women's Health (.gov) +2", 'Where to find help: If you are struggling with your mental health, resources like Postpartum Support International offer specialized directories, peer support, and professional guidance to help you navigate this transition.', 'When to call a doctor: Reach out to your healthcare provider immediately if you experience heavy bleeding (soaking a pad per hour), a high fever, severe abdominal pain, or thoughts of harming yourself or your baby.']

all_items: ['Rodent Contact: You usually get infected by breathing in dust contaminated with the virus while cleaning, working, or playing in enclosed spaces (like sheds or cabins) where rodents have nested.', 'Person-to-Person: Human-to-human transmission is extremely rare. However, the Andes virus strain (primarily found in South America) is the only type known to spread between people through very close, prolonged contact.', 'No Animal Bites: While rare, you can also catch it if bitten by an infected rodent or by eating contaminated food. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention | CDC (.gov) +3', 'Hantavirus Pulmonary Syndrome (HPS):', 'Hemorrhagic Fever with Renal Syndrome (HFRS):', 'Early Stage: Starts with flu-like symptoms, including fever, chills, severe muscle aches, fatigue, and sometimes headaches or stomach issues.', 'Advanced Stage: Rapidly worsens into respiratory issues or kidney complications, characterized by coughing, shortness of breath, and chest tightness. YouTube·Associated Press', 'Treatment: There is no specific cure, antiviral medication, or vaccine. Treatment relies on intensive care, hospitalization, and mechanical ventilation to help patients breathe while their bodies fight the virus.', 'Prevention: The best defense is avoiding contact with rodents. This includes sealing holes in homes, eliminating easy food sources for pests, and using proper protective measures (like masks and gloves) when cleaning rodent-infested areas. YouTube·CBC News +2']

all_items: ['The Incident: On May 2, 2026, a cluster of severe respiratory illnesses was reported aboard the MV Hondius.', 'The Strain: Cases were traced to the Andes virus (ANDV), which is predominantly found in South America. It is the only hantavirus strain capable of person-to-person transmission.', 'Case Count: The outbreak was contained with 11 confirmed/probable cases and 3 deaths.', 'Response: Passengers were safely disembarked, medically evacuated, and quarantined in their home countries. World Health Organization (WHO) +5', 'Transmission: Primarily contracted through inhalation of aerosolized urine, feces, or saliva of infected wild rodents.', 'Symptoms: Early signs mimic severe flu—fever, chills, headache, and muscle aches—rapidly progressing to severe shortness of breath and respiratory failure.', 'Global Situation: Aside from the ship outbreak, isolated cases remain rare but continue to occur globally through direct contact with rodents or their habitats. World Health Organization (WHO) +3', 'Avoid Dry Sweeping: Never sweep or vacuum rodent-infested areas dry, as this kicks up infectious particles. Instead, dampen the area first.', 'Rodent Control: Seal openings in homes and workplaces, and store food in secure, airtight containers.', 'Medical Attention: If you suspect you have been exposed to rodents or a confirmed case and develop respiratory symptoms, seek immediate emergency medical evaluation. World Health Organization (WHO) +1']

all_items: ['Mild to Moderate Cases: Symptoms typically clear within a week or two. While infectiousness drops rapidly, individuals can still spread the virus for about 10 days from symptom onset.', 'Severe Cases: Illness can stretch for weeks or even months, and may require hospitalization.', 'Lingering Effects: Even after testing negative, a lingering cough, fatigue, or changes in taste/smell may persist for several more weeks.', 'Definition: The World Health Organization defines Long COVID as symptoms that continue or develop three months after the initial infection and last for at least two months.', 'Duration: Most people with Long COVID eventually see improvement within 12 to 18 months.', 'Common Symptoms: Severe fatigue, brain fog, shortness of breath, and joint pain are among the most frequently reported ongoing issues. Cleveland Clinic +2', 'Definition: Major health bodies define Long COVID as a condition where symptoms are continuous, relapsing/remitting, or progressive, and cannot be explained by an alternative diagnosis.', 'Causes: Research suggests it acts as an infection-associated chronic condition, potentially driven by persistent viral reservoirs, ongoing immune system dysregulation, or inflammation.', 'Prevalence: While the condition can affect anyone exposed to the virus, studies show it impacts roughly 10–20% of people who get infected. World Health Organization (WHO) +2']

all_items: ['Extreme Fatigue: Often described as a "crashing" exhaustion or severe lethargy that fails to improve with rest, and can worsen after minimal physical or mental exertion.', 'Cognitive Impairment: Frequently referred to as "brain fog," this includes difficulty concentrating, memory loss, and a general feeling of mental sluggishness.', 'Respiratory & Heart Issues: Persistent shortness of breath, chronic cough, chest pain, and heart palpitations (racing or pounding heartbeat).', 'Neurological Symptoms: Frequent headaches, sleep disturbances (insomnia), dizziness upon standing (lightheadedness), and "pins-and-needles" sensations.', 'Digestive Problems: Abdominal pain, nausea, diarrhea, and loss of appetite.', 'Sensory and Body Aches: Joint or muscle pain, rashes, hair loss, and the loss or change of taste and smell. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention | CDC (.gov) +7']

all_items: ['Fatigue: Pacing your physical and mental activity is key. Some clinical trials suggest certain medications, like the antidepressant fluvoxamine, may significantly help ease persistent fatigue.', 'Heart Symptoms: Conditions like Postural Orthostatic Tachycardia Syndrome (POTS) or heart palpitations are often managed with increased hydration, compression garments, and prescription medications such as beta-blockers.', 'Pain & Headaches: Over-the-counter pain relievers or prescription neuromodulators may be used depending on the type and severity of muscle and joint pain.', 'Breathing Difficulties: Pulmonary rehabilitation, breathing exercises, and inhalers are commonly utilized to improve shortness of breath.', 'Loss of Smell or Taste: Olfactory retraining—frequently smelling distinct essential oils and scents—is the recommended method for regaining these senses. Cleveland Clinic +5', 'Energy Pacing: Recognizing your energy limits and resting before symptoms worsen is vital, especially if you experience post-exertional malaise (where symptoms spike after exertion).', 'Rehabilitation: Physical, occupational, and speech therapy can be extremely helpful if long COVID has affected your physical strength, mobility, or cognitive functions.', 'Supportive Habits: Eating a balanced, anti-inflammatory diet, staying hydrated, and utilizing stress-reduction techniques can support overall systemic recovery. Mayo Clinic +2']

all_items: ['LI4 (Hegu - The Hand Valley): Located on the back of the hand between the thumb and index finger. Commonly used to treat headaches, toothaches, and neck pain.', 'ST36 (Zusanli - Leg Three Miles): Positioned below the knee on the outer front leg. Used to boost energy, enhance immunity, and relieve gastrointestinal issues.', 'PC6 (Neiguan - Inner Gate): Found on the inner forearm, about two finger-widths below the wrist crease. Widely used to reduce nausea, motion sickness, and anxiety.', 'LR3 (Taichong - Great Surge): Located on the top of the foot in the webbing between the big toe and second toe. Targeted to relieve stress, menstrual cramps, and high blood pressure.', 'GV20 (Baihui - Hundred Meetings): Situated at the very top of the center of the head. Used to calm the mind, improve focus, and relieve headaches. Precision Healing and Wellness', 'Anatomical Landmarks: Using bones, tendons, and muscle junctures.', 'Proportional Measurement: Using a proportional "body inch" system ( $Cun$), where the width of a patient\'s thumb represents $1 \\text{ cun}$ and the width of four joined fingers equates to $3 \\text{ cun}$.', 'Palpation: Searching for subtle depressions, skin temperature variations, or areas of tenderness (the sensation is sometimes a dull ache known in traditional practice as De Qi). chiro.org +2']

all_items: ['Turmeric: Known for its potent anti-inflammatory properties, curcumin (the active compound in turmeric) is often used to manage arthritis pain.', 'Ginger: Extensively used to alleviate nausea, motion sickness, and digestive discomfort.', 'Ginkgo Biloba: Traditionally used to improve blood circulation and address issues like ringing in the ears (tinnitus).', 'Echinacea: Frequently used to stimulate the immune system and help the body fight off colds and mild respiratory infections.', "St. John's Wort: Studied as a remedy for mild to moderate depression, anxiety, and insomnia. Healthline +2", "Drug Interactions: Herbs can interfere with prescription medications. For example, St. John's Wort can lower the effectiveness of certain statins and antidepressants.", 'Regulation & Dosage: Herbal supplements are not always subject to the same strict manufacturing and purity standards as conventional drugs.', 'Toxicity: Certain herbs, like comfrey and chaparral, carry risks of liver toxicity. Wikipedia +1']

all_items: ['Medical Abortion: Often referred to as the "abortion pill," this involves taking medications (such as mifepristone and misoprostol) to end a pregnancy. It is typically used in the early stages and mimics the process of a heavy period.', 'Surgical Abortion: A procedure performed by a healthcare provider in a clinic or hospital setting. It uses gentle suction or specific medical instruments to safely remove the pregnancy tissue from the uterus. World Health Organization (WHO) +3', 'Induced vs. Spontaneous: While "abortion" medically encompasses both, conversational use of the word almost always refers to induced abortion (a deliberate choice to end a pregnancy). A pregnancy that ends naturally on its own is referred to as a miscarriage, or spontaneous abortion.', 'Not Contraception: An abortion is used to end an existing pregnancy, whereas birth control (like condoms, pills, or IUDs) prevents pregnancy from happening. Wikipedia +2', 'World Health Organization (WHO): Recognized globally as essential healthcare, safe abortion care is backed by comprehensive guidelines available through the World Health Organization Abortion Fact Sheet.', 'Medical Guidance: For clear, step-by-step information on what a procedure entails, consult resources like the MedlinePlus Abortion Procedure Overview.']

all_items: ['Mifepristone: Taken first, this medication stops the pregnancy from growing by blocking progesterone.', 'Misoprostol: Taken 24 to 48 hours later, this medication causes cramping and bleeding to empty your uterus.', 'Recovery: Cramping and heavy bleeding usually peak a few hours after taking the second pill and subside once the pregnancy passes. Bleeding can last 1–2 weeks. Cleveland Clinic +3', 'Common side effects: Heavy cramps, bleeding, blood clots, nausea, diarrhea, and mild fever/chills.', 'Effectiveness: It is roughly 99% effective. A small percentage of people (2–5%) may require a follow-up procedure if the abortion is incomplete. Better Health Channel +1', 'Excessive bleeding: Soaking through more than two large sanitary pads per hour for two hours in a row.', "Severe, unmanageable pain or pain that doesn't improve after the pregnancy tissue has passed.", 'A high fever ( $>100.4^{\\circ }F$ or $38^{\\circ }C$) that persists for more than 24 hours.', 'Foul-smelling vaginal discharge or severe abdominal tenderness.']

all_items: ['The MTP Act: Abortions are legally regulated by the Medical Termination of Pregnancy (MTP) Act, which generally permits termination up to 20 weeks for all women, and up to 24 weeks for specific vulnerable categories including minors, rape survivors, and women with disabilities.', 'Marital Status Equality: In a landmark verdict ( X v. Principal Secretary ), the Supreme Court ruled that unmarried women have the exact same rights to abortion care as married women up to 24 weeks, declaring that marital status cannot be a basis for limiting reproductive choice.', 'Late-Term & Minor Interventions: In cases involving minor rape survivors seeking to terminate pregnancies beyond standard legal limits (such as 30 to 31 weeks), the Court has repeatedly granted permissions. The judiciary has clearly established that a minor cannot be forced into carrying a pregnancy to term against her will, bypassing strict procedural delays. National Institutes of Health (.gov) +7', 'Official Laws: To understand the exact legalities and gestational parameters, review the Medical Termination of Pregnancy Act.', 'Legal Context: For more details on the breakdown of historic and evolving precedents, read Supreme Court Observer.']

all_items: ['What it is: A virus targeting the immune system. Left untreated, it progresses to AIDS (Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome).', 'No Cure, but Manageable: With proper medical care and Antiretroviral Therapy (ART), HIV can be controlled. People with suppressed viral loads can live long, healthy lives and cannot transmit the virus to partners. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention | CDC (.gov) +1', 'Acute Infection: 2 to 4 weeks after exposure, many people experience flu-like symptoms (e.g., fever, rash, sore throat, swollen lymph nodes), while others have no symptoms at all.', 'Clinical Latency (Chronic HIV): The virus continues to multiply but at very low levels. Without treatment, this stage can last a decade or longer.', 'AIDS: The most advanced stage where the immune system is severely damaged. People with AIDS have low CD4 counts and are highly susceptible to severe opportunistic infections. all_items: ['HIV: The virus that attacks the immune system. Not everyone with HIV has AIDS.', 'AIDS: The final, most severe stage of HIV infection. It is diagnosed when the immune system becomes severely compromised. HIV.gov +1', 'Have a CD4 T-cell count drop below $200$ cells per cubic millimeter of blood (standard counts are $500$ to $1600$).', 'Are diagnosed with one or more specific "AIDS-defining" opportunistic infections or cancers. Clinical Info HIV.gov +1', 'Common transmission methods: Unprotected sexual contact or sharing injection drug equipment.', 'No transmission risk: HIV cannot be transmitted through casual day-to-day contact like kissing, hugging, sharing food, or shaking hands. World Health Organization (WHO) +1', 'Effectiveness: When taken as prescribed, ART can reduce the amount of virus in the blood to undetectable levels.', 'Benefits: This viral suppression allows the immune system to recover, prevents progression to AIDS, and stops the virus from being transmitted to sexual partners (often referred to as $U=U$, or Undetectable = Untransmittable). HIV.gov +2']

all_items: ['Undetectable = Untransmittable (U=U): When taken exactly as prescribed, ART reduces your viral load to undetectable levels. At this stage, the risk of sexually transmitting HIV to a negative partner is virtually eliminated.', 'Medication Classes: ART usually combines three different medicines from at least two drug classes to prevent the virus from building drug resistance. Common classes include NRTIs, NNRTIs, and Integrase Inhibitors. National Institutes of Health (.gov) +3', 'Format: Most treatments are taken as a single, once-daily pill, though some patients use long-acting injectables given once a month or every other month.', 'Consistency: Strict adherence to your medication schedule is essential to maintain viral suppression. National Institutes of Health (.gov) +3', 'Manageable: While modern HIV medications are highly effective and have been refined to cause far fewer side effects, some people may experience issues like nausea, fatigue, or sleep disturbances.', 'Monitoring: Doctors track your progress through routine blood work, specifically testing your viral load and CD4 T-cell count. Mayo Clinic +1', 'Start Immediately: Treatment should begin as soon as possible after diagnosis.', 'Official Resources: To find local care clinics, calculate treatment costs, or learn more about navigating your diagnosis, refer to the NIH HIVinfo Treatment Guidelines or the CDC HIV Treatment Overview.']

all_items: ['Vaginoplasty / Vulvoplasty: The main genital procedure. It involves removing the penis and testicles to construct a functional vagina (vaginoplasty) or solely external female genitalia (vulvoplasty).', 'Facial Feminization Surgery (FFS): Softens facial features, which can include a tracheal shave (Adam’s apple reduction), jaw contouring, and forehead reconstruction.', 'Breast Augmentation: Enhances breast size and contour through implants or fat grafting.', 'Other Procedures: Can include body contouring (buttock augmentation), and voice feminization surgery to adjust pitch. Johns Hopkins Medicine +2', 'Care Standards: Medical guidelines generally require a formal diagnosis of gender dysphoria and letters of recommendation from licensed mental health professionals before proceeding with surgery.', 'Hormone Replacement Therapy (HRT): Many individuals undergo feminizing HRT (estrogen and anti-androgens) for a significant period to promote tissue changes prior to surgery.', 'Recovery: Genital procedures usually require a 3 to 5-day hospital stay. Recovery involves several weeks of limited physical activity and, in the case of vaginoplasty, a strict, daily routine of vaginal dilation to prevent the neovagina from closing or narrowing. National Institutes of Health (.gov) +2']

query: how does gender reassignment surgery work
all_items: ['Transmasculine: Involves removing breast tissue (mastectomy) and reshaping the chest to create a masculine appearance. Common techniques include double incision or peri-areolar (keyhole) methods.', 'Transfeminine: Involves breast augmentation using implants to create a feminine breast contour.', 'Transmasculine: This often involves a Phalloplasty (constructing a penis using tissue from another part of the body) or a Metoidioplasty (enlarging existing clitoral tissue). It may also include a vaginectomy (removal of the vagina) and scrotoplasty (creation of a scrotum).', 'Transfeminine: Typically involves a Vaginoplasty, which uses existing genital tissue (like penile and scrotal skin) to construct a vaginal canal, clitoris, and labia. A simpler variation called vulvoplasty creates the external vulva but no vaginal canal. Johns Hopkins Medicine +4', 'These procedures reshape the face to align with a person’s gender identity. This can include jaw contouring, chin reshaping (genioplasty), rhinoplasty, and forehead reconstruction.
